🟡 These light wood shavings have retained their pine smell and fluidity.
🟡 A pile of clean wood sawdust, free of dust and foreign matter.

common.fun_fact: This fine sawdust from untreated wood is extremely rich in carbon and very poor in nitrogen. It must be mixed with wet nitrogenous materials to balance. It absorbs excess moisture and serves as a growth medium of choice for wood-decaying fungi that break down lignin, enriching the soil over time and enhancing crop yields.
common.name: Clean wood sawdust

badly damaged: 🔴 Dry straw strands blackened and spongy, crumbling into tiny organic debris.
🔴 These straw stalks are colonized by dark molds and covered with silt.
🔴 Straw debris in advanced decomposition, black and deeply embedded in soil.
damaged: 🟠 Damp straw strands showing bends and gray moisture spots on the surface.
🟠 These stalks have lost their golden luster and present signs of rot.
🟠 Softened straw strands having lost their original stiffness under water.
worn: 🟡 Very golden and firm dry straw strands, recently discarded in the garden.
🟡 These cereal stalks have retained their tubular shape and crisp texture.
🟡 A batch of clean straw strands, free of any traces of sticky soil.

common.fun_fact: These dry cereal stalks provide a massive amount of carbon in the form of cellulose and hemicellulose. They create important air spaces that promote the breathing of aerobic bacteria. Their slow decomposition by fungi helps to structure the soil and retain water, preventing drought damage to crops nearby.
common.name: Dry straw strands

badly damaged: 🔴 A houseplant reduced to rotten black stems and slimy, decaying roots.
🔴 This plant residue is covered with a thick coating of white and gray mold.
🔴 A decomposed green plant forming a shapeless spongy organic mass.
damaged: 🟠 A withered houseplant with yellow, drooping and dry leaves on the stems.
🟠 This plant shows softened stems and rot spots around the root collar.
🟠 Worn green plant remains whose roots are beginning to dry out completely.
worn: 🟡 A dry but whole houseplant, discarded recently after wilting.
🟡 This dead plant has retained its structure of dried stems and leaves.
🟡 A clean houseplant showing dry potting soil residues on its roots.

common.fun_fact: This dead plant combines nitrogen-rich leaves and carbonaceous fibrous stems. Its degradation returns to the soil the mineral salts and trace elements it had stored during its life. Its dry roots provide a porous texture that helps aeration and the development of beneficial soil microorganisms in the ground.
common.name: Withered houseplant

badly damaged: 🔴 Plum pits blackened and split, crumbling into tiny black fragments in the soil.
🔴 These plum pits are covered with black mold and a slippery silt layer.
🔴 Broken plum pit debris embedded in damp clayey mud at the bottom.
damaged: 🟠 Plum pits showing deep cracks and brown soil accumulations on the surface.
🟠 These pits have lost their original brown color and show signs of wear.
🟠 Softened fruit remains still containing a few whole plum pits inside.
worn: 🟡 Whole and very dry plum pits, recently discarded after consumption.
🟡 These small brown seeds have retained their characteristic hardness and grooves.
🟡 A batch of clean plum pits, without any trace of stuck fruit flesh.

common.fun_fact: These hard pits have a very dense woody shell that resists moisture. They decompose slowly under the action of microscopic fungi. They enrich the soil with carbon and serve as temporary shelters for small terrestrial invertebrates that participate in the fragmentation of decaying organic matter in the earth.
common.name: Plum pits

badly damaged: 🔴 A shriveled and dried lemon peel, covered with a thick layer of green mold.
🔴 This lemon residue has turned completely black and hardened, almost fossilized.
🔴 Shreds of spongy lemon peel decomposing slowly in the damp mud.
damaged: 🟠 A softened lemon peel showing whitish discolorations on its outer zest.
🟠 This piece of skin has lost its bright yellow color and presents torn edges.
🟠 A withered lemon peel whose white inner pith has completely dissolved.
worn: 🟡 A still fresh and fragrant lemon peel, discarded after being squeezed.
🟡 This lemon skin has kept its beautiful yellow color despite a slight drying.
🟡 A clean lemon peel showing neat knife cut marks on the surface.

common.fun_fact: This yellow peel contains acidic oils and cellulose. Although citrus initially slows down bacteria, its volatile compounds evaporate quickly in the air. It then provides carbon and vitamins useful to soil fungi that finish decomposing it cleanly, enriching the ground with important nutrients over time.
common.name: Lemon peel

badly damaged: 🔴 Hazelnut shells blackened and split into tiny, almost undetectable pieces.
🔴 These hazelnut casings are covered with a slippery green silt and are rotting.
🔴 Hazelnut shell fragments deeply embedded in the damp clayey mud.
damaged: 🟠 Hazelnut shells showing deep cracks and brown soil accumulations on the surface.
🟠 These shell pieces have lost their hardness and start to crumble under water.
🟠 Empty hazelnut half-shells having lost their original light brown color.
worn: 🟡 Very dry, whole hazelnut shells, recently discarded after consumption.
🟡 These woody shells have retained their characteristic rounded shape and stiffness.
🟡 A pile of clean hazelnut shells, showing clean, sharp fracture lines.

common.fun_fact: These small, very hard wooden casings are rich in carbon and lignin. They take many months to break down under the slow action of wood-decaying fungi. They play an important structural role in the earth by preventing compaction and improving the infiltration of rainwater into the deeper soil layers.
common.name: Hazelnut shells

badly damaged: 🔴 Tomato vines reduced to blackened woody filaments and rotten moldy fibers.
🔴 This plant residue is in advanced decay, covered with gray mycelium.
🔴 Tomato vine debris melted into a shapeless, spongy organic mush.
damaged: 🟠 Withered and softened tomato vines, showing brown rot spots along the stems.
🟠 These stems present significant tears and a loss of fiber rigidity.
🟠 Worn plant remains whose green stalks are starting to dry and turn yellow.
worn: 🟡 Dry but whole tomato vines, discarded after harvesting the fruits.
🟡 These green branches have retained their tomato foliage smell and fuzzy texture.
🟡 A batch of clean tomato vines, showing neat shear cuts at the ends.

common.fun_fact: These long, fibrous stems contain cellulose, lignin, and nitrogen. They decompose in a few weeks under the effect of filamentous fungi. Their degradation returns potassium and calcium to the soil, while providing a fibrous structure that maintains terrestrial aeration for optimal microflora growth in the soil.
common.name: Tomato vines
Brosse à dents usagée

badly damaged: 🔴 A toothbrush with its nylon bristles completely torn off and the handle split.
🔴 This composite plastic handle is broken in two and covered in stubborn limescale.
🔴 A piece of rigid, curved plastic, the remains of a faded brush handle.
damaged: 🟠 A toothbrush with synthetic bristles ruffled and flattened from long use.
🟠 The ergonomic handle shows gray wear marks and tiny surface scratches.
🟠 This oral hygiene utensil displays shallow cracks on its non-slip rubber grip.
worn: 🟡 A used toothbrush still whole, with its bristles slightly bent on the sides.
🟡 This bi-color plastic handle is slightly faded from bathroom humidity.
🟡 A recently discarded toothbrush, its bristles still clean but somewhat worn.

common.fun_fact: The standard toothbrush combines multiple insoluble plastic types, including a rigid polypropylene handle and heat-fused nylon bristles. This intimate fusion of different polymers makes industrial recycling impossible. Over time, it slowly fragments into persistent microplastics that pollute waterways, contaminate groundwater, and accumulate in the tissues of delicate aquatic wildlife.

common.name: Tesson de céramique

badly damaged: 🔴 A shard of glazed terracotta reduced to a dull, polished pebble-like state.
🔴 This piece of broken dishware is cracked in several places and covered in black silt.
🔴 A ceramic shard whose painted design has become completely invisible due to erosion.
damaged: 🟠 A sharp fragment originating from a white porcelain plate or bowl.
🟠 This piece of earthenware shows a clean break with very sharp edges.
🟠 A piece of glazed ceramic cup, with traces of sediment on the corners.
worn: 🟡 A large piece of stoneware tile with its glossy glaze almost intact.
🟡 This modern pottery shard shows slight scratches on its colored surface.
🟡 A fragment of a decorated cup that still retains its handle despite the fall.

common.fun_fact: Shards of earthenware, porcelain, and stoneware have a melting temperature much higher than standard packaging glass. If mixed with recyclable bottles, they create major structural defects known as unfused stones, making new bottles fragile and highly prone to breaking. Therefore, these inert ceramic fragments must be separated from glass streams and sent to appropriate disposal sites.

common.name: Miroir brisé

badly damaged: 🔴 Small silvered glass shards clumped together in silty, dark sand.
🔴 This mirror fragment has lost almost all of its reflective metallic backing.
🔴 A residue of flat glass, opaque and yellowed by slippery organic deposits.
damaged: 🟠 A mirror sheet broken in a star pattern with deep transverse cracks.
🟠 This reflective glass piece shows sharp splinters and flaking silvering.
🟠 A piece of a broken pocket mirror whose surface is tarnished by silt.
worn: 🟡 A worn but flat piece of mirror that still reflects a clear image underwater.
🟡 This mirror debris displays superficial scratches without major silvering loss.
🟡 A portion of reflective glass with only its corners slightly dulled.

common.fun_fact: The glass used for mirrors is a specific flat glass whose chemical composition differs significantly from standard packaging glass. Additionally, its back is coated with a protective metallic silvering layer that disrupts the operations of recycling furnaces. This composite waste cannot be melted down with ordinary bottles and must be permanently disposed of via residual collection streams.

common.name: Couche jetable

badly damaged: 🔴 A disposable diaper swollen with river water, torn open and leaking its internal gel.
🔴 This hygiene product is half-decomposed, covered in foul-smelling silt.
🔴 Shreds of white cellulose and elastic plastic caught beneath a submerged branch.
damaged: 🟠 A baby diaper saturated with water, its adhesive strips completely torn away.
🟠 This disposable protection shows exposed, soiled synthetic cotton fibers.
🟠 A used diaper that has become heavy after stagnating at the bottom of the water.
worn: 🟡 A whole but wet disposable diaper, recently discarded and carried by currents.
🟡 This infant diaper shows crumpling marks and slight outer discoloration.
🟡 A used diaper discarded intact, still retaining its plastic outer structure.

common.fun_fact: A disposable diaper is a complex assembly of wood pulp cellulose, superabsorbent sodium polyacrylate, and multiple protective polypropylene or polyethylene films. This inseparable mixture of wet organic material and petroleum-based plastics makes mechanical sorting impossible, requiring direct incineration with energy recovery or placement in long-term waste containment facilities.

common.name: Lingette désinfectante

badly damaged: 🔴 A synthetic wipe frayed into a clump of sticky, gray fibers.
🔴 This used wipe is embedded with black silt and wrapped tightly around a root.
🔴 A piece of torn non-woven fabric, almost translucent under the effect of water.
damaged: 🟠 A cleaning wipe full of holes, having lost its whiteness and artificial scent.
🟠 This disposable fabric shows fraying along the edges and greasy stains.
🟠 A used wet wipe partially deformed by the strength of the river current.
worn: 🟡 A disinfectant wipe discarded whole, still flexible but gray with dust.
🟡 This rectangular piece of non-woven material shows minor wrinkles without tears.
🟡 A worn cleaning wipe washed up on the wet sand of the riverbank.

common.fun_fact: Unlike toilet paper made of pure cellulose, cleaning wipes consist of non-woven synthetic fibers, often polyester or viscose. They do not dissolve in water and create massive blockages in sewerage networks, trapping other harmful materials and blocking pumps at treatment plants before they are eventually screened out and sent to the incinerator for final disposal.

common.name: Boîte à pizza grasse

badly damaged: 🔴 Softened pieces of brown cardboard, reduced to an oily, dark pulp.
🔴 A shredded cardboard box whose internal surface is black with mold.
🔴 A portion of corrugated cardboard box in advanced decay, stuck to river weeds.
damaged: 🟠 A pizza box warped by water, with large rings of congealed grease.
🟠 This cardboard lid shows major tears and stuck-on cheese residues.
🟠 A cardboard package folded in half, saturated with brackish water and smelling rancid.
worn: 🟡 A whole but flattened pizza box showing cooking oil stains.
🟡 This corrugated cardboard shows slight dampness with its printed logos still legible.
🟡 A recently discarded pizza box, dry on top but stained on the inside.

common.fun_fact: Cardboard soiled by food grease or vegetable oils cannot be recycled by traditional paper mills. The fats do not separate from the cellulose fibers during the pulping process, which spoils the quality of the recycled paper pulp and results in structural holes in the new paper sheets. This dirty cardboard packaging must therefore be disposed of with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Litière de chat souillée

badly damaged: 🔴 A pile of clay granules clumped into a foul-smelling, shapeless gray mud.
🔴 This wet mineral litter has dissolved into a sticky sediment at the river bottom.
🔴 Hardened clay blocks mixed with plant debris and dark silt.
damaged: 🟠 A broken clump of clumping clay litter releasing dusty grayish grains.
🟠 This mineral litter shows dried urine stains and emits an ammonia odor.
🟠 Silica granules saturated with water, having completely lost their absorbing power.
worn: 🟡 A handful of recently discarded mineral litter granules, still coarse.
🟡 This pile of absorbent gravel shows minor dampness without dissolving.
🟡 Pet litter composed of small grains of clumped gray bentonite.

common.fun_fact: Mineral pet litter is primarily made of swelling clays like bentonite or silica gel. Once soiled by animal waste, it harbors pathogenic parasites like Toxoplasma gondii. Its inorganic nature combined with its heavy bacterial load prevents any biological processing by composting or standard material recycling, making its disposal in landfill or incineration systems necessary.

common.name: Sac d'aspirateur plein

badly damaged: 🔴 A thick paper bag torn open, releasing clumps of compact gray dust.
🔴 This non-woven filter package is ruptured, revealing a mixture of hair and silt.
🔴 Shreds of brown cellulose fibers saturated with a putrid black mud.
damaged: 🟠 A vacuum cleaner bag swollen with water, its cardboard seams peeling apart.
🟠 This filtering envelope shows punctures and is leaking fine indoor dust.
🟠 A dusty collection bag whose rigid plastic collar is broken or warped.
worn: 🟡 A whole vacuum bag discarded full, heavy with compressed domestic debris.
🟡 This multi-layer paper filter pocket shows superficial damp stains.
🟡 A used dust collection bag whose thick cardboard ring is still intact.

common.fun_fact: The vacuum cleaner bag is made of kraft paper lined with non-woven synthetic fibers to trap microparticles, plus a glued plastic or cardboard fixing ring. This multi-material design, combined with the unknown and often toxic nature of the vacuumed household dust, prohibits any recycling of its structure, requiring its disposal along with unsorted residual waste.

common.name: Mégot de cigarette

badly damaged: 🔴 A yellowed, frayed cellulose acetate filter releasing fine synthetic fibers.
🔴 This cigarette remnant is crushed, blackened by water, and half-decomposed.
🔴 A tiny cylinder of synthetic fibers embedded in sticky bottom silt.
damaged: 🟠 A damp cigarette butt with pieces of brown paper wrapping still attached.
🟠 This used filter shows dark tar stains and emits a strong stale tobacco odor.
🟠 A cigarette butt warped by water, its protective paper wrapping unrolling.
worn: 🟡 A recently discarded whole cigarette butt, its orange paper wrapper intact.
🟡 This used filter displays minor fading from sunlight without losing its shape.
🟡 A smoked cigarette thrown into the water, its remaining tobacco now dissolved.

common.fun_fact: A single cigarette butt contains thousands of toxic chemical substances like nicotine, cadmium, and lead trapped in its cellulose acetate filter. This plastic takes over ten years to fragment, polluting up to five hundred liters of water and poisoning delicate aquatic organisms. This hazardous chemical waste must be collected and disposed of permanently via non-recyclable streams.

common.name: Chewing-gum mâché

badly damaged: 🔴 A hardened, black ball of gum embedded with sand grains and tiny gravel.
🔴 This chewing gum is flattened, faded by water, and covered with a film of silt.
🔴 A grayish elastic residue, stuck and fossilized onto a rough river stone.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of soft, deformed chewing gum showing visible tooth impressions.
🟠 This chewed gum has lost its original flavor and shows a dull pink color.
🟠 A sticky chewing gum covered in fine river silt and small algae strands.
worn: 🟡 A recently chewed gum, still flexible and white or green in color.
🟡 This piece of elastic gum shows minor surface wrinkles without embedded debris.
🟡 A worn ball of gum floating between two water layers after being spat out.

common.fun_fact: Modern chewing gum is manufactured from a synthetic gum base composed of elastomers, waxes, and petroleum-derived polymers similar to polyethylene. Completely non-biodegradable and impossible to sort or recycle due to its sticky nature and small size, it must be incinerated with residual waste to prevent long-term accumulation and contamination in the environment.

common.name: Stylo à bille usé

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic pen broken into several pieces, lacking its ink refill and cap.
🔴 This transparent pen body is yellowed by the sun and clogged with mud.
🔴 A split plastic tube whose ink cartridge has leaked into the surrounding water.
damaged: 🟠 A scratched ballpoint pen with its metallic tip oxidized by humidity.
🟠 This writing instrument shows cracked plastic and an outdated ink supply.
🟠 A plastic pen whose push-button mechanism is broken or blocked by sand.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn plastic ballpoint pen, its writing tip still protected.
🟡 This clear pen shows a few superficial scratches on its plastic shirt clip.
🟡 A used pen whose outer plastic is slightly dull but not deformed.

common.fun_fact: The ballpoint pen is a complex assembly of polystyrene for the rigid body, polypropylene for the cap, and a tungsten carbide tip set in brass. Its small size and the presence of hydrophobic grease-based ink residues prevent its detection and processing in standard sorting centers for packaging plastics. This multi-material product must be disposed of with general residual waste.

common.name: Ticket de caisse

badly damaged: 🔴 A piece of thermal paper blackened and reduced to shapeless shreds.
🔴 This receipt is almost completely decomposed, forming a sticky gray paste.
🔴 A fragment of thin paper receipt whose ink has washed away in the water.
damaged: 🟠 A crumpled receipt showing large gray spots from thermal chemical reaction.
🟠 This receipt shows significant tears and a texture softened by moisture.
🟠 A store ticket folded in four whose printed text has become illegible.
worn: 🟡 A whole but crumpled store receipt, recently discarded on the wet ground.
🟡 This receipt shows minor folding marks without chemical alteration of the paper.
🟡 A thermal ticket whose printed characters are still partially visible.

common.fun_fact: Receipts are made of thermal paper coated with a chemical dye that reacts to heat. This coating often contains bisphenol A or S, endocrine disruptors that would contaminate the water recycling loop if this paper were processed together with clean paper and cardboard. To protect other paper recycling streams, it must be discarded with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Cendres de bois

badly damaged: 🔴 A deposit of fine ashes diluted in silty water, forming a gray residue.
🔴 This combustion dust has clumped together into a compact alkaline paste.
🔴 Powdery charcoal residues mixed with muddy bottom sediments.
damaged: 🟠 A pile of gray wood ashes still containing small pieces of charred wood.
🟠 These fireplace remains show significant dampness and small hardened lumps.
🟠 Fly ash scattered at the bottom of the water, forming a grayish veil.
worn: 🟡 A batch of dry, powdery chimney ashes, light gray in color.
🟡 These burnt wood residues show fine, unaltered black charcoal debris.
🟡 Recently discarded wood ashes, still light and dry before getting wet.

common.fun_fact: Ashes from wood heating or barbecues concentrate heavy metals naturally absorbed by trees during their growth, along with polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from incomplete combustion. These powdery residues cannot be recycled and must be discarded cold in general household waste to prevent the spread of toxic dust and chemical contamination in water systems.

common.name: Éponge synthétique

badly damaged: 🔴 A shredded kitchen sponge, reduced to a heap of shapeless synthetic foam.
🔴 This cleaning utensil is blackened by mold and encrusted with greasy silt.
🔴 Crumbled pieces of polyurethane foam that disintegrate when touched.
damaged: 🟠 A synthetic sponge with its green abrasive scouring side half torn off.
🟠 This foam block shows major tears and embedded organic debris.
🟠 A used sponge warped by humidity and hardened by crystallized marine salt.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn dishwashing sponge, faded yellow or pink in color.
🟡 This cleaning tool displays minor signs of wear on its abrasive side.
🟡 A recently discarded sponge, still elastic despite a few gray stains.

common.fun_fact: The synthetic kitchen sponge is usually made of polyurethane foam glued to an abrasive scouring web of polyester fibers loaded with phenolic resins. This glued bi-material structure cannot be recycled and ultimately releases toxic microplastics during its environmental degradation. It must be discarded along with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Rasoir jetable

badly damaged: 🔴 A razor handle broken in two, missing its metal head and yellowed by salt.
🔴 This plastic razor shows steel blades that are completely rusted and detached.
🔴 A molded rigid plastic fragment covered in silt and decayed vegetation.
damaged: 🟠 A disposable razor with orange rust forming on its steel blades.
🟠 This blue razor shows a scratched handle and a degraded lubricating strip.
🟠 A used shaving tool whose plastic head is cracked or slightly deformed.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn disposable razor, lacking its clear plastic protective cover.
🟡 This plastic handle shows minor surface scratches without blade corrosion.
🟡 A recently discarded plastic razor, still clean despite a slightly dull finish.

common.fun_fact: The disposable razor is a perfect example of a non-recyclable multi-material product. It combines a rigid polystyrene handle, a lubricating strip of water-soluble polymers, and stainless steel blades. Separating these materials on an industrial scale is economically impossible. Thus, it must be routed to standard household incineration for final disposal.

common.name: Coton-tige usagé

badly damaged: 🔴 A white plastic stick bent at a right angle, lacking cotton tips.
🔴 This cotton swab is flattened, yellowed, and embedded in smelly black silt.
🔴 A frayed rigid plastic stick caught between two small river stones.
damaged: 🟠 A used cotton swab with ruffled, soiled cotton fibers on its ends.
🟠 This plastic stem shows bending stress marks and slight discoloration.
🟠 A damp cotton swab whose body of plastic or compressed paper is softening.
worn: 🟡 A cotton swab discarded whole, its cotton tips still relatively compact.
🟡 This small stick shows minor handling marks without structural failure.
🟡 A white plastic cotton swab whose cotton is slightly gray with dust.

common.fun_fact: Plastic-stemmed cotton swabs were historically one of the most common wastes found in aquatic environments. Too thin to be intercepted by wastewater treatment grids, they pass through filters and float in seas before being ingested by seabirds who mistake these sticks for food. They must be placed in secure household disposal streams.

common.name: Masque chirurgical

badly damaged: 🔴 A protective mask in blue shreds, with its elastic loops broken.
🔴 This medical device is half-buried in the silt, covered with dark sediments.
🔴 Pieces of blue non-woven fabric shredded by the actions of the water.
damaged: 🟠 A crumpled surgical mask with mud accumulated on its pleated surface.
🟠 This mask shows a bent metal nose wire and a stretched-out elastic loop.
🟠 A used disposable respiratory filter whose blue color has faded in the sun.
worn: 🟡 A whole but wet surgical mask floating near the water surface.
🟡 This protective mask shows minor wrinkles without tears on the filter fabric.
🟡 A recently discarded mask whose elastic ear loops are still intact.

common.fun_fact: The disposable surgical mask is made of non-woven polypropylene, a synthetic plastic obtained by melt-blowing. Its structure also integrates a steel nose clip sheathed in plastic and spandex elastics. This mix of metals and polymers makes recycling complex and financially unviable, requiring permanent destruction through thermal waste treatment.

common.name: Gants en latex troués

badly damaged: 🔴 A fragment of natural vulcanized rubber decomposing beneath riverbed stones.
🔴 This glove is completely torn into strips stuck together by dark silt.
🔴 A piece of hardened and cracked elastic latex, reduced to a yellow skin.
damaged: 🟠 A latex glove with major punctures at the fingertips.
🟠 This protective glove shows traces of black dirt and chemical discoloration.
🟠 An elastic disposable glove stretched out and filled with wet, fine sand.
worn: 🟡 A whole latex glove discarded after use, pale yellow or white in color.
🟡 This hand protection shows minor wrinkles without any visible tears.
🟡 A worn thin rubber glove that still retains its original hand-like shape.

common.fun_fact: Although latex comes from the sap of the rubber tree, disposable gloves undergo sulfur vulcanization and contain chemical additives to improve elasticity. These treatments prevent biological composting or mechanical recycling, requiring thermal incineration alongside general household waste to prevent chemical and biological contamination.

common.name: Brosse à cheveux

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic hairbrush with its handle broken off and all bristles ripped out.
🔴 This brush body is encrusted with dried algae, silt, and tangled hair.
🔴 A molded plastic fragment, the remnant of a hairbrush faded by water exposure.
damaged: 🟠 A hairbrush with bent plastic pins and a cracked rubber cushion base.
🟠 This plastic brush shows deep surface scratches and stubborn limescale build-up.
🟠 A worn hair styling tool whose ergonomic handle is warped or deformed.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn hairbrush, missing only a few plastic bristles.
🟡 This plastic model shows minor scratches on its back without major damage.
🟡 A recently discarded hairbrush still containing a few tangled strands of hair.

common.fun_fact: A hairbrush often combines an ABS rigid plastic body, a flexible elastomer cushion, and nylon or metal bristles tipped with epoxy resin. This intimate mix of glued materials cannot be separated for plastic recycling and must be permanently disposed of in the general household waste streams for mechanical destruction.

common.name: Poussière de balayage

badly damaged: 🔴 Fine dust residue diluted in muddy water, forming a grayish deposit.
🔴 This dust pile has clumped into a compact paste saturated with organic debris.
🔴 Household sweepings dissolved at the water bottom, forming sterile silt.
damaged: 🟠 A grayish pile of sweepings containing fabric fibers and small breadcrumbs.
🟠 This dust shows large mixed debris and dark clumps of hair.
🟠 Damp domestic cleaning residues, forming soft balls of gray fibers.
worn: 🟡 A dry and powdery pile of sweepings, recently accumulated from floor cleaning.
🟡 This residue shows fine sand grains mixed with fabric lint.
🟡 Dry sweepings discarded in a packet, containing microscopic fibers.

common.fun_fact: Sweeping dust is a concentration of human dander, pet hair, synthetic microfibres from carpets and clothing, soot, and outdoor particles. This heterogeneous and chemical-heavy mixture cannot be recovered by biological composting or mechanical recycling and must be thrown into general household waste to prevent wind dispersal.

common.name: Cheveux et poils

badly damaged: 🔴 A clump of hair decomposed into a black, slimy felt covered in silt.
🔴 This pet fur is amalgamated with river bottom clay, forming a sticky mass.
🔴 Frayed capillary fibers embedded in decaying organic debris.
damaged: 🟠 A mass of tangled hair forming a wet ball full of fine sand.
🟠 This dog or cat fur shows residues of dirt and decayed leaves.
🟠 A hair clump twisted by water eddies, holding small pieces of wood.
worn: 🟡 A recently discarded clump of cut hair, still flexible and dry.
🟡 This ball of pet fur displays well-preserved natural color shades.
🟡 A clean capillary cluster floating gently adrift between water layers.

common.fun_fact: Although made of biodegradable keratin, human hair and pet fur collected in the domestic environment are often contaminated with chemical cosmetics, silicones, and shampoo residues. When mixed with household waste, they must be disposed of via standard non-recyclable streams to avoid contaminating organic composting cycles.

common.name: Pot en terre cuite brisé

badly damaged: 🔴 A shard of flowerpot eroded into a dull, orange clay pebble.
🔴 This piece of terracotta is cracked in several places and covered in silt.
🔴 A porous pottery fragment encrusted with green algae and white limescale.
damaged: 🟠 A sharp fragment originating from a red clay flowerpot.
🟠 This terracotta piece shows a break with sharp angles and stuck-on potting soil.
🟠 The base of an unglazed ceramic pot, displaying mineral salt deposits.
worn: 🟡 A large piece of flowerpot with its curved rim still recognizable.
🟡 This terracotta shard shows minor soil traces without surface erosion.
🟡 A recently broken garden pottery fragment, bright orange in color.

common.fun_fact: Terracotta is clay baked at high temperatures that has lost its ability to become malleable again in water. This inert material cannot be recycled with glass packaging due to its high melting point, nor blended with plastics, requiring it to be disposed of as residual waste in appropriate containment facilities.

common.name: Vaisselle en porcelaine

badly damaged: 🔴 A shard of white tableware reduced to a small, dull, polished pebble.
🔴 This plate fragment has network cracks and is covered in black mud.
🔴 A piece of porcelain whose decorative gold trim has completely vanished in water.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of a broken plate with edges that are sharp like glass.
🟠 This cup debris shows significant cracks across its glazed surface.
🟠 A porcelain shard showing floral patterns that are partially worn away.
worn: 🟡 A recently broken piece of tableware, its white surface still very shiny.
🟡 This saucer debris shows minor sand rubbing marks on its top glaze.
🟡 A fragment of a porcelain plate, clean but broken along its edges.

common.fun_fact: Porcelain is a fine ceramic obtained from kaolin fired at over fourteen hundred degrees. This vitrified material has a highly stable crystalline structure that does not melt in traditional glass-making furnaces. It constitutes a major pollutant for the glass recycling industry and must be thrown in with general non-recyclable rubbish.

common.name: Coton démaquillant

badly damaged: 🔴 A makeup remover pad frayed into a clump of sticky white fibers.
🔴 This used cotton is embedded with black silt and smells of decomposition.
🔴 A torn cotton fiber residue, almost shapeless after a long stay in the water.
damaged: 🟠 A used makeup remover pad showing dark makeup stains and product residues.
🟠 This cotton disc displays stretched fibers and accumulated dirt particles.
🟠 A piece of hydrophilic cotton saturated with gray river water.
worn: 🟡 A whole but damp makeup remover cotton pad, discarded after single use.
🟡 This piece of cotton shows minor surface wrinkles without edge tears.
🟡 A worn cosmetic cotton pad washed up on the wet sand near the water.

common.fun_fact: Although cotton is a natural plant fiber, used makeup remover pads contain residues of chemical cosmetics, solvents, heavy metals from makeup, and microplastics. Furthermore, they are often chlorine-bleached, which excludes them from organic composting options and requires secure disposal in the general non-recyclable waste stream.

common.name: Ruban adhésif usé

badly damaged: 🔴 A shriveled ball of plastic adhesive tape, blackened by stuck-on dust.
🔴 This tape is shredded into tiny curled-up pieces that cling to each other.
🔴 A fragment of adhesive plastic film permanently stuck to a piece of driftwood.
damaged: 🟠 A dirty piece of adhesive tape with its glue layer full of sand grains.
🟠 This tape shows tears and has completely lost its original transparency.
🟠 A used adhesive strip displaying major crumpling and dark stains.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn strip of adhesive tape, recently discarded.
🟡 This self-adhesive plastic shows a slight loss of stickiness without tears.
🟡 A piece of clear tape, crumpled and damp, floating in the water.

common.fun_fact: Standard adhesive tape consists of a backing made of polypropylene or PVC coated with a thermoplastic adhesive based on synthetic rubber and tackifying resins. This blend of solvents and thin plastics makes it incompatible with plastic or cardboard recycling loops, requiring it to be thrown out with general non-recyclable refuse.

common.name: Papier sulfurisé usé

badly damaged: 🔴 A brown baking sheet in softened pieces, blackened by burns and oil.
🔴 This parchment paper is torn and covered in black food molds.
🔴 A sticky baking paper residue in advanced decomposition under the silt.
damaged: 🟠 A baking paper warped by moisture with stains from baked-on grease.
🟠 This paper shows charred edges and burnt dough residues stuck to it.
🟠 A parchment sheet folded into a ball, full of river water and smelling rancid.
worn: 🟡 A whole but crumpled sheet of parchment paper, stained with yellow grease.
🟡 This baking paper shows heavy fold lines without any major tearing.
🟡 A worn parchment paper discarded recently, dry on one side and damp on the other.

common.fun_fact: Parchment paper, or baking paper, is treated with sulfuric acid for water resistance and coated with a thin layer of silicone to make it non-stick. This silicone polymer impregnation prevents the wood fibers from dissolving during standard paper recycling processes, requiring it to be placed in general household waste.

common.name: Paille en plastique

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic straw split lengthwise, flattened and bleached by marine salt.
🔴 This flexible tube is broken into several segments clogged with black silt.
🔴 A thin cylindrical plastic piece, the remnant of a faded drinking straw.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic straw scratched all over, with its accordion elbow torn or warped.
🟠 This drinking tube shows bite marks and has lost its bright colors.
🟠 A plastic tube showing fine sediment deposits on its inner walls.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn red or blue plastic straw, recently discarded.
🟡 This plastic tube shows superficial scratches without major warping.
🟡 A used straw whose rigid structure is preserved despite floating in the water.

common.fun_fact: Standard plastic straws are made of polypropylene. Because of their hollow cylindrical shape and low weight, they often escape automatic optical sorting systems. They represent a direct threat to aquatic wildlife, which can ingest them by accident. They must be disposed of with general non-recyclable household rubbish.

common.name: Film étirable souillé

badly damaged: 🔴 A mass of cling wrap crumpled into a tight ball, black with mud and mold.
🔴 This thin plastic is shredded into sticky scraps that are impossible to unroll.
🔴 Flexible plastic filaments wrapped around roots and embedded with silt.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic cling wrap showing tears and stuck-on dried food residues.
🟠 This thin envelope shows greasy gray stains and trapped condensation.
🟠 A used food wrap folded in half, sticky and covered with damp sand.
worn: 🟡 A piece of cling wrap whole but crumpled onto itself, discarded after use.
🟡 This flexible plastic shows minor dampness without major alteration.
🟡 A worn food wrap floating like a transparent membrane between water layers.

common.fun_fact: Plastic food cling wrap is made of low-density polyethylene or flexible PVC. Its extreme thinness causes it to wrap around drive shafts and rotors in sorting centers, leading to costly mechanical breakdowns. Therefore, it must be excluded from standard packaging recycling and thrown into the non-recyclable garbage.

common.name: Emballage de bonbon

badly damaged: 🔴 A metallized candy wrapper torn into tiny, faded plastic shreds.
🔴 This packaging shows a crumpled plastic surface embedded with black silt.
🔴 A piece of shiny plastic film whose printed logos have washed off in water.
damaged: 🟠 A folded and sticky candy wrapper displaying tears along its sides.
🟠 This shiny plastic shows dried sugar spots and accumulated dust.
🟠 A used confectionery bag warped and containing dirty river water.
worn: 🟡 A whole but empty candy wrapper, its metallic colors still bright.
🟡 This sachet shows minor superficial wrinkles without any major holes.
🟡 A recently discarded plastic wrapper floating flat near the bank.

common.fun_fact: Confectionery wrappers are often made of complex films integrating polypropylene and a thin layer of metallized aluminum to protect the candy from moisture. This type of multi-layer composite plastic film cannot be separated industrially for recycling purposes and must be placed in general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Bouchon synthétique

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic cork split in two, yellowed and covered with an algal crust.
🔴 This cylindrical cork shows extreme wear marks and crumbles when touched.
🔴 A residue of hardened expanded plastic embedded in canal bottom mud.
damaged: 🟠 A synthetic bottle cork with deep corkscrew insertion marks.
🟠 This beige plastic cylinder shows deformations and dark damp stains.
🟠 An imitation cork stopper showing chemical discoloration and embedded sand.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn synthetic wine cork displaying light surface scratches.
🟡 This model shows superficial traces of use without alteration of its cylindrical shape.
🟡 A recently discarded bottle stopper, still elastic and clean.

common.fun_fact: Synthetic corks are manufactured from expanded polyethylene to mimic the flexible texture of natural cork. Although entirely plastic, their small cylindrical shape prevents them from being captured by automated sorting centers for standard household packaging. They must be thrown away with general unsorted household refuse.

common.name: Pinceau en nylon usé

badly damaged: 🔴 A brush with no bristles, its plastic handle broken and covered in dried paint.
🔴 This tool shows a rusted and bent metal ferrule, which is almost detached.
🔴 A faded paintbrush handle buried under a layer of sediment and dark mud.
damaged: 🟠 A paintbrush with its nylon bristles clumped together by dry varnish residues.
🟠 This painting tool displays structural warping on its plastic body.
🟠 A worn brush showing oxidation on its metal binding strip.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn paintbrush with its synthetic bristles slightly ruffled.
🟡 This model shows dry paint splatters without any handle cracks.
🟡 A recently discarded paintbrush, its bristles hard but the structure intact.

common.fun_fact: A standard paintbrush unites a plastic or varnished wood handle, a steel or tinplate ferrule, and nylon bristles glued with epoxy resins. This combination of synthetic materials, metals, and insoluble adhesive agents prevents any material recycling or composting. It must be discarded with general non-recyclable household rubbish.

common.name: Fil dentaire usagé

badly damaged: 🔴 A frayed dental floss reduced to a tangle of grayish nylon fibers.
🔴 This plastic strand is blackened by mud and encrusted with marine sediment.
🔴 A strip of synthetic floss tied tightly around a piece of driftwood.
damaged: 🟠 A used dental floss showing fraying along its length and discoloration.
🟠 This strand shows tension wrinkles and has lost its original white color.
🟠 A segment of nylon thread covered in fine, sticky river silt.
worn: 🟡 A piece of dental floss whole but wrinkled, discarded after use.
🟡 This thread shows minor dampness without major fiber separation.
🟡 A worn nylon dental floss floating gently adrift between water layers.

common.fun_fact: Dental floss is made from extruded nylon or teflon filaments, often waxed with synthetic polymers and flavored. These high-tensile technical plastics do not degrade and easily wrap around the fins of aquatic animals, causing severe injuries. They must be safely thrown into the non-recyclable garbage.

common.name: Papier photo glacé

badly damaged: 🔴 A photo in softened pieces, its plastic emulsion layer peeling off.
🔴 This glossy paper is torn, yellowed, and covered in thick black mold.
🔴 A photo print fragment whose image has completely disappeared under water.
damaged: 🟠 A photo paper warped by moisture, with major structural curling.
🟠 This paper shows cracking on its glossy side and accumulated mud spots.
🟠 A worn print displaying chemical discoloration of its original color tones.
worn: 🟡 A whole but crumpled photo paper showing minor surface scratches.
🟡 This printed photo shows superficial dampness on its cardboard backing.
🟡 A worn glossy print discarded recently, its image remaining visible despite the water.

common.fun_fact: Glossy photo paper is coated with a receptive layer of plastic resins like polyethylene, and sometimes metallic oxides to fix inks. This composite plastic-paper formulation prevents the recovery of cellulose fibers during standard paper recycling processes, requiring its disposal along with unsorted residual waste.

common.name: Papier peint arraché

badly damaged: 🔴 Softened shreds of wallpaper, reduced to a gray pulp full of adhesive.
🔴 This tapestry element is torn into pieces covered with thick black mold.
🔴 A fragment of wall covering in advanced decay beneath river pebbles.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of wallpaper warped with dried adhesive residues on its back.
🟠 This paper shows major tears and significant fading of its patterns.
🟠 A worn tapestry strip displaying gray plaster traces and mud stains.
worn: 🟡 A whole but crumpled piece of wallpaper, discarded during renovations.
🟡 This paper shows minor wrinkles without major loss of its color coating.
🟡 A worn wall covering strip, damp on its cardboard backing side.

common.fun_fact: Modern wallpaper contains mineral fillers, synthetic inks, and is often coated with a protective PVC film. Additionally, it is saturated with water-insoluble methylcellulose adhesive residues. These additives prevent it from being recycled alongside clean papers and require it to be thrown out with general waste.

common.name: Mouchoir en papier

badly damaged: 🔴 A paper tissue softened to the extreme, forming a shapeless fibrous paste.
🔴 This disposable paper is torn into tiny scraps stuck to the sediment.
🔴 A fine cellulose residue almost dissolved by the action of the current.
damaged: 🟠 A crumpled tissue showing dirt traces and a loss of its original whiteness.
🟠 This paper shows major tears and a structure weakened by water absorption.
🟠 A used tissue folded into a ball, saturated with damp gray silt.
worn: 🟡 A whole but wet paper tissue, recently discarded in the grass.
🟡 This thin paper shows minor wrinkles without transverse tearing.
🟡 A worn tissue thrown away recently, still white despite a few water spots.

common.fun_fact: Although made of wood fibers, paper tissues are treated with wet-strength resins to prevent them from tearing during use. Additionally, when soiled with biological fluids, they pose a biohazard that excludes them from recycling loops alongside clean packaging cardboard. They must be discarded with general household refuse.

common.name: Coquilles d'huîtres

badly damaged: 🔴 A shell fragment broken into small, sharp, dull calcareous pieces.
🔴 This shell is cracked, covered in foul black silt and dried algae.
🔴 A fossilized calcareous debris blackened by riverbed sediments.
damaged: 🟠 An oyster shell with significant chips on its sharp, rugged edges.
🟠 This mollusk remnant shows soil stains and irregular calcareous deposits.
🟠 An empty shell showing discoloration on its pearly internal surface.
worn: 🟡 A whole and solid oyster shell, its original shape well-defined.
🟡 This calcareous remnant shows minor wear marks without edge breaks.
🟡 A clean shell discarded recently, displaying natural gray color tones.

common.fun_fact: Oyster and mollusk shells are composed of calcium carbonate in the form of calcite and aragonite, structured by an organic matrix. This dense calcareous material does not melt during glass recycling and must be incinerated or discarded with general residual waste to prevent damage to recycling machinery.

common.name: Sac de charbon de bois

badly damaged: 🔴 A thick kraft paper bag torn into shreds soaked in black charcoal dust.
🔴 This packaging shows a cardboard surface reduced to a black coaly pulp.
🔴 A piece of blackened kraft bag whose structure disintegrates in the silt.
damaged: 🟠 A crumpled charcoal bag blackened on the inside by fine carbon dust.
🟠 This packaging shows major tears at its base and damp, soiled areas.
🟠 A heavy paper bag displaying fragments of broken synthetic closing thread.
worn: 🟡 A whole but empty charcoal bag, dull brown in color with black logos.
🟡 This double-layer paper bag shows minor wrinkles without major holes.
🟡 A worn kraft bag discarded recently, dry on one side and blackened on the other.

common.fun_fact: The charcoal bag is made of heavy multi-ply kraft paper, often glued with synthetic adhesives and blackened by pure carbon dust. These carbon residues spoil the paper pulp during recycling, making its disposal via standard household waste streams necessary to protect paper reclamation facilities and keep municipal processing smooth.

common.name: Plat Pyrex brisé

badly damaged: 🔴 Small shards of thick, transparent glass clumped together in river sand.
🔴 This piece of tempered glass baking dish shows whitish internal cracks.
🔴 A residue of borosilicate glass, opaque and flaked by aquatic erosion.
damaged: 🟠 A baking dish plate broken diagonally with razor-sharp edges.
🟠 This transparent Pyrex fragment shows clean breaks and accumulated silt.
🟠 A piece of broken kitchen dish whose surface is scratched by the current.
worn: 🟡 A worn but flat piece of Pyrex dish, retaining its original transparency.
🟡 This glass debris shows minor surface scratches without internal fractures.
🟡 A baking dish fragment with only its corners chipped or cracked.

common.fun_fact: Pyrex is a borosilicate glass specially treated to withstand thermal shocks. Its melting temperature is much higher than that of ordinary glass. If discarded in bottle recycling bins, it does not melt completely in the furnaces, ruining entire batches of recycled glass. It must be thrown away with general household waste.

common.name: Verre à boire cassé

badly damaged: 🔴 Fine glass shards mixed with dark, sandy riverbed sediments.
🔴 This drinking glass base is broken into sharp pieces covered in silt.
🔴 A residue of hollow transparent glass, polished by rolling pebbles.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of drinking glass displaying sharp, transverse breaks.
🟠 This transparent glass fragment shows very sharp edges and stuck clay.
🟠 Table glass debris worn down, with its upper rim cracked or chipped.
worn: 🟡 A large piece of drinking glass broken recently, still clean and bright.
🟡 This glass debris shows minor wear marks on its foot or stem.
🟡 A fragment of transparent glass tumbler without chemical discoloration.

common.fun_fact: Tableware glass is often crystal or soda-lime glass enriched with lead or barium oxide for shine. Its chemical composition prevents it from being recycled alongside glass packaging, as it alters the viscosity of the molten glass in industrial furnaces. It must be discarded with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Paquet de café vide

badly damaged: 🔴 A crumpled, silver plastic coffee bag, black with mud and torn open.
🔴 This flexible packaging shows an internal surface covered in moldy grounds.
🔴 A residue of stretched plastic film bag whose welded seam has ruptured.
damaged: 🟠 A warped coffee bag showing heavy folding marks and accumulated dust.
🟠 This metallized wrap shows minor tears and a damaged plastic valve.
🟠 A used coffee bag folded in half, retaining gray river water inside.
worn: 🟡 A whole but empty coffee bag, its shiny metallic surface well-preserved.
🟡 This flexible sachet shows minor scratches without holes on its back.
🟡 A recently discarded coffee packaging floating flat near the riverbank.

common.fun_fact: The coffee bag is a multi-layer composite packaging that combines a PET outer layer, an aluminum barrier middle layer, and a PE sealing inner layer. This metal-plastic sandwich is impossible to separate efficiently in standard sorting and recycling streams, requiring its final disposal with non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Barquette en polystyrène

badly damaged: 🔴 A white tray broken into spongy pieces that crumble in the water.
🔴 This expanded plastic is blackened by mud and encrusted with slimy weeds.
🔴 A fragment of spongy polystyrene yellowed by the sun and half-decomposed.
damaged: 🟠 A polystyrene tray warped by water, showing dried meat blood stains.
🟠 This lightweight packaging shows clean fractures and missing sections.
🟠 A used foam tray displaying major compression wrinkles and wet sand.
worn: 🟡 A whole but empty white tray, recently washed up on the riverbank.
🟡 This expanded packaging shows minor scratches without structural breakage.
🟡 A tray made of lightweight foam plastic floating adrift between water layers.

common.fun_fact: The meat or fish tray is made of expanded polystyrene. Composed of ninety-eight percent air, this material is bulky and difficult to transport economically to recycling plants. Soiled with fats and organic juices, it also presents hygiene risks, requiring its collection with general household waste for incineration.

common.name: Feutre usagé desséché

badly damaged: 🔴 A broken plastic felt-tip pen, lacking its colored tip and rear cap.
🔴 This yellow pen body is faded by the sun and clogged with dark silt.
🔴 A split plastic tube whose internal ink pad has dried out or leaked.
damaged: 🟠 A scratched felt pen with its writing nylon tip flattened or split.
🟠 This drawing instrument shows cracked plastic and complete lack of ink.
🟠 A worn felt-tip pen whose plastic cap is blocked by dry river mud.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn colored felt pen, its tip dry and depleted of ink.
🟡 This plastic pen shows minor surface scratches without cracks on the barrel.
🟡 A used felt pen whose plastic is slightly dull but not deformed.

common.fun_fact: The felt-tip pen combines a polypropylene body, a writing tip made of sintered nylon or polyester, and an internal reservoir of cellulose acetate fibers soaked in chemical solvent-based ink. This mix of fine plastics and hydrophobic inks makes it non-recyclable, requiring its disposal in the general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Règle en plastique cassée

badly damaged: 🔴 A transparent plastic ruler broken into several sharp, jagged fragments.
🔴 This ruler piece is yellowed by the sun, scratched, and covered in black silt.
🔴 A rigid polystyrene debris whose measurement markings have vanished in water.
damaged: 🟠 A ruler fragment displaying significant transverse cracks along its length.
🟠 This broken ruler shows sharp splinters and embedded fine sand grains.
🟠 A worn measuring tool whose printed graduations are partially rubbed off.
worn: 🟡 A plastic ruler whole but snapped in the middle, recently discarded.
🟡 This ruler piece shows superficial scratches without major discoloration.
🟡 A fragment of rigid graduated plastic, its edges polished by the current.

common.fun_fact: Rigid plastic rulers are manufactured from crystal polystyrene. This brittle plastic easily fragments under the mechanical action of waves and currents, releasing angular microplastics that harm aquatic wildlife. Lacking a dedicated recycling stream, these broken rulers must be disposed of with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Brosse à vaisselle usée

badly damaged: 🔴 A dishwashing brush with its plastic handle snapped and nylon bristles torn off.
🔴 This brush body is covered in limescale, black silt, and congealed grease.
🔴 A split rigid plastic handle, the remnant of a faded cleaning brush.
damaged: 🟠 A dishwashing brush with synthetic bristles ruffled and flattened from use.
🟠 This plastic handle shows deep scratches and gray greasy deposits.
🟠 A worn cleaning tool whose plastic brush head is cracked or deformed.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn dishwashing brush, its bristles slightly bent on the sides.
🟡 This model shows minor signs of use without cracks on its handle.
🟡 A recently discarded dish brush, still clean despite a dull plastic finish.

common.fun_fact: The dishwashing brush combines a rigid polypropylene or nylon handle with synthetic scrubbing bristles set in metal steel staples. This inseparable multi-material design prevents its recycling alongside sorted household plastic packaging. It must therefore be discarded with residual waste to be permanently disposed of via thermal incineration facilities.

common.name: Gant de ménage troué

badly damaged: 🔴 A household cleaning glove made of cracked yellow rubber melted by the sun.
🔴 This glove is torn into shreds stuck together by sticky black silt.
🔴 A remnant of hardened thick latex, covered with porous marine sediments.
damaged: 🟠 A rubber glove with significant tears on the palm and fingertips.
🟠 This household glove shows dark chemical stains and embedded sand.
🟠 A worn yellow latex glove, stretched out and filled with damp gray mud.
worn: 🟡 A whole but punctured household glove, showing minor crumpling.
🟡 This hand protection shows minor signs of wear without major discoloration.
🟡 A worn yellow rubber glove discarded recently, dry on the inside.

common.fun_fact: Household rubber gloves are made of vulcanized natural latex or synthetic nitrile. They undergo chemical treatments to resist detergents and are lined with cotton flocking. This composite mix makes chemical or material recycling impossible, requiring their disposal in the general non-recyclable household waste to prevent contamination.

common.name: Élastique en caoutchouc

badly damaged: 🔴 A rubber band dry and cracked, snapping at the slightest stretch.
🔴 This elastic band is yellowed by the sun and stuck to a piece of dead algae.
🔴 A fragment of decomposed vulcanized rubber, embedded in the mud.
damaged: 🟠 A rubber band warped and stretched out, showing tiny micro-cracks.
🟠 This binder shows dirt stains and has lost its original elasticity.
🟠 A worn brown rubber band covered in fine silt and sand grains.
worn: 🟡 A rubber band whole but worn, recently discarded after use.
🟡 This binder shows minor tension marks without any visible breakage.
🟡 A clean but slightly loose brown rubber band, still flexible.

common.fun_fact: The natural rubber band contains latex vulcanized with sulfur and stabilizing mineral fillers. This vulcanization process creates irreversible chemical bonds that prevent the material from melting down, making technical recycling impossible and requiring its disposal with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Punaise de bureau

badly damaged: 🔴 A broken plastic pushpin, missing its metallic pin and yellowed by water.
🔴 This tool shows a steel pin that is completely rusted and bent.
🔴 A metal pushpin embedded in a hard aggregate of sand and rust.
damaged: 🟠 A pushpin displaying red rust on its steel pin.
🟠 This colored plastic model shows cracks and surface scratches.
🟠 A used pushpin presenting minor deformations on its plastic cap.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn pushpin, bright yellow or red in color.
🟡 This metallic pin shows minor oxidation marks without major corrosion.
🟡 A recently discarded plastic pushpin, still sharp and clean.

common.fun_fact: The office pushpin combines a tempered steel pin with a grip cap made of polystyrene or molded plastic. The tiny size of this waste and the intimate combination of metal and plastic prevent its mechanical sorting in automated centers, requiring its final disposal with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Bouchon de stylo usagé

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic pen cap broken in two and yellowed by the sun.
🔴 This cap shows deep bite marks and is clogged with dark silt.
🔴 A piece of rigid and split plastic, the remains of a faded pen cap.
damaged: 🟠 A pen cap with its pocket clip snapped off or warped.
🟠 This blue cap shows sand rubbing scratches and accumulated mud.
🟠 A plastic cap showing fine limescale deposits on the inside.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn plastic pen cap, recently discarded.
🟡 This transparent cap shows minor surface scratches without major cracks.
🟡 A used pen cap whose plastic is clean but slightly dull.

common.fun_fact: The pen cap is generally manufactured from polypropylene. Due to its very small size, it falls through the grids of mechanical sorting systems for standard packaging and ends up as sorting residue. It must be disposed of with general residual waste to ensure proper containment and prevent microplastic pollution.

common.name: Pochette plastique déchirée

badly damaged: 🔴 A thin plastic sheet protector crumpled into a ball and black with mold.
🔴 This clear plastic is shredded into sticky scraps full of dark silt.
🔴 Strips of perforated plastic wrapped tightly around a wet root.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic sheet protector displaying tears along its edges and sand.
🟠 This thin envelope shows gray stains and a partially detached binder strip.
🟠 A used protector folded in half, retaining dirty river water inside.
worn: 🟡 A whole but crumpled sheet protector, discarded after office use.
🟡 This transparent plastic shows minor wrinkles without tears on its faces.
🟡 A worn plastic sleeve floating gently like a sail in the water.

common.fun_fact: Perforated plastic sheet protectors are made of thin polypropylene or PVC. Their lack of rigidity and tendency to tangle in rotating mechanical sorting screens cause major service interruptions. This is why they are not accepted in standard recycling streams and must end up with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Carte de crédit périmée

badly damaged: 🔴 A bank card cut into several pieces, faded and damaged by water.
🔴 This rigid plastic piece shows a rusted, peeling metal microchip.
🔴 A yellowed plastic card fragment whose magnetic strip is crumbling.
damaged: 🟠 An expired credit card with significant cracks and embedded sand.
🟠 This plastic card shows deep scratches and deformation from heat exposure.
🟠 A used card cut in half, holding deposits of fine river silt.
worn: 🟡 A whole but expired credit card showing minor superficial scratches.
🟡 This plastic card shows light wear on its printed paint without cracks.
🟡 A recently discarded bank card, still rigid despite floating in the water.

common.fun_fact: The bank card is composed of multiple PVC layers fused under high pressure, integrating a copper antenna for contactless payment, a silicon microchip, and a magnetic strip of iron oxide. This complex plastic-metal composite cannot be recycled through standard channels and must be thrown in with general non-recyclable trash.

common.name: Badge d'accès plastique

badly damaged: 🔴 An access badge broken into several rigid fragments yellowed by water.
🔴 This plastic badge shows a rusted and bent metal attachment clip.
🔴 A piece of rigid plastic card clogged with dark river bottom silt.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic badge with cracks and its printed logo partially rubbed off.
🟠 This access card shows sand scratches and fine crystallized salt deposits.
🟠 A worn badge displaying thermal warping along with fine silt traces.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn plastic access badge, missing its fabric lanyard.
🟡 This card shows minor superficial scratches without major breakage.
🟡 A recently discarded plastic badge, still rigid and clean.

common.fun_fact: Access badges often contain an RFID chip and a copper coil antenna embedded in PVC or polycarbonate plastic. This mixture of fine electronics and rigid plastic prevents them from being integrated into standard recycling systems for plastic packaging, requiring their disposal in general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Jouet en plastique cassé

badly damaged: 🔴 A decapitated plastic figurine, missing arms and bleached by marine salt.
🔴 This broken toy shows sharp edges and is clogged with black mud.
🔴 A split rigid plastic toy fragment, covered in algae and sediments.
damaged: 🟠 A small plastic toy with bent limbs or missing structural parts.
🟠 This colorful toy shows deep sand scratches and significant fading.
🟠 A worn toy displaying oxidation traces on its tiny steel assembly screws.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn plastic toy, dull green or red in color.
🟡 This plastic model shows minor wear marks without any structural cracks.
🟡 A small toy discarded recently, still clean despite tired-looking plastic.

common.fun_fact: Plastic toys are made from diverse polymers like ABS, polycarbonate, or PVC, and often contain chemical flame retardants and heavy metal stabilizers. This heterogeneous mixture of plastic formulas excludes them from recycling loops alongside standard packaging, requiring their final disposal with unsorted residual waste.

common.name: Coque de téléphone silicone

badly damaged: 🔴 A silicone phone case completely torn in two and yellowed by the sun.
🔴 This cover shows black burn marks and sticky, dark river silt.
🔴 A warped piece of flexible elastomer encrusted with mud and algae.
damaged: 🟠 A scratched phone case with its molded button covers torn or missing.
🟠 This silicone cover shows greasy stains and embedded sand grains.
🟠 A used cover displaying a loss of elasticity and a dull overall color.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn silicone phone case displaying minor wrinkles.
🟡 This flexible model shows light rubbing marks on its corners.
🟡 A case discarded recently, still flexible despite some moisture traces.

common.fun_fact: The silicone used for protective covers is a synthetic elastomer composed of silicon, oxygen, carbon, and hydrogen. This three-dimensionally crosslinked polymer does not melt under normal heat and cannot be recycled alongside thermoplastics like PET or PE. It must therefore be thrown in with general non-recyclable refuse.

common.name: Emballage de préservatif

badly damaged: 🔴 An aluminum condom wrapper torn into tiny, water-faded pieces.
🔴 This packaging shows a crumpled metal surface embedded with black silt.
🔴 A fragment of shiny foil whose printed lettering has completely vanished.
damaged: 🟠 A folded and dirty condom wrapper displaying tears along its sides.
🟠 This square sachet shows lubricant stains and fine sand grains stuck to it.
🟠 A used metallic sachet warped and holding dirty river water inside.
worn: 🟡 A whole but empty condom wrapper, dull gold or red in color.
🟡 This sachet shows minor superficial wrinkles without major holes on its back.
🟡 A recently discarded metallic wrapper floating flat near the bank.

common.fun_fact: The condom wrapper is a composite film combining aluminum and polyethylene plastic layers to guarantee absolute airtightness and light protection. This type of multi-layer composite packaging cannot be separated for recycling and must be safely disposed of along with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Préservatif usagé

badly damaged: 🔴 A yellowed, slimy elastic latex, reduced to a shapeless bag full of sand.
🔴 This rubber product is torn into pieces stuck together by foul silt.
🔴 A thin latex residue in advanced decay beneath a submerged bottom stone.
damaged: 🟠 A used condom swollen with water, showing traces of chemical lubricant.
🟠 This latex protection displays dirt accumulation and large tension wrinkles.
🟠 A flexible worn latex, filled with damp gray mud and fine algae strands.
worn: 🟡 A whole used condom, dull white in color, floating between water layers.
🟡 This rubber shows minor superficial moisture without any tears.
🟡 A latex condom discarded recently, still retaining its elastic structure.

common.fun_fact: Used condoms are made of vulcanized natural rubber latex or synthetic polyurethane, lubricated with silicone oils. Soiled with biological fluids, they present a hygiene hazard and can only be disposed of via standard household waste incineration systems along with general non-recyclable refuse to protect public sanitation.

common.name: Pansement usagé

badly damaged: 🔴 A peeled-off adhesive strip, frayed into a clump of sticky gray fibers.
🔴 This used bandage is embedded with black silt and smells rancid.
🔴 A piece of adhesive plastic whose central gauze pad has detached in water.
damaged: 🟠 A used bandage displaying dried blood stains and accumulated dirt.
🟠 This adhesive tape shows stretched fibers and glue filled with sand.
🟠 A crumpled and damp pink plaster, partially peeled off by water action.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn adhesive bandage, discarded after single use.
🟡 This piece of sticky fabric shows minor wrinkles without tears.
🟡 A worn pink plaster washed up on the wet sand near the water edge.

common.fun_fact: The adhesive bandage is a composite of polyurethane plastic film or woven fabric, acrylic glue, and a viscose-polyester pad. Soiled with blood and biological agents, its hybrid and infectious nature prohibits any material recycling or composting, requiring its disposal in general household waste destined for incineration.

common.name: Restes de bougie

badly damaged: 🔴 A wax block warped and blackened by soot, embedded with coarse sand.
🔴 This candle remnant shows crumbled wax around a charred wick.
🔴 A paraffin debris yellowed by the sun and half-dissolved beneath pebbles.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of candle wax displaying fractures and accumulated mud spots.
🟠 This block shows remnants of a blackened cotton wick and fine silt.
🟠 A worn candle remnant whose original cylindrical shape is warped.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn piece of candle wax, white or red in color.
🟡 This block shows minor signs of wear without major fractures in the wax.
🟡 A worn wax candle discarded recently, clean despite a few surface scratches.

common.fun_fact: Modern candles are primarily made of paraffin, a crude oil distillate solid at room temperature, or stearin. This hydrophobic wax does not decompose naturally in water and cannot be recycled with household plastics due to its low melting point. It must therefore be thrown in with general non-recyclable household rubbish.

common.name: Lunettes de vue cassées

badly damaged: 🔴 An eyeglass frame broken into several pieces, completely missing its lenses.
🔴 This plastic frame shows metal hinges that are rusted and warped.
🔴 A fragment of thick optical lens, scratched and embedded in dark bottom silt.
damaged: 🟠 Eyeglasses with a cracked lens and a frame scratched by sand.
🟠 This frame shows a missing temple arm and persistent limescale traces.
🟠 A worn pair of glasses displaying significant warping along the bridge.
worn: 🟡 Eyeglasses whole but broken at the hinge, recently discarded.
🟡 This model shows minor scratches on the lenses without losing transparency.
🟡 A worn pair of spectacles whose lenses are dirty but otherwise intact.

common.fun_fact: Corrective eyeglasses combine optical lenses made of organic polymer glass and a complex frame made of cellulose acetate or metal with steel screws. This multi-material structure cannot be sorted by automated recycling centers, requiring its final disposal with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Peigne en plastique cassé

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic comb with all its teeth broken off and the handle split.
🔴 This comb body is yellowed by the sun and encrusted with black silt.
🔴 A piece of rigid, serrated plastic, the remains of a faded comb.
damaged: 🟠 A comb with several missing teeth and deep surface scratches.
🟠 This plastic comb shows gray wear marks and persistent limescale deposits.
🟠 A worn hair styling tool whose plastic structure is warped or bent.
worn: 🟡 A plastic comb whole but missing one or two teeth along its length.
🟡 This plastic model shows minor surface scratches without major cracks.
🟡 A used comb discarded recently, still clean despite a dull plastic finish.

common.fun_fact: The plastic comb is generally manufactured by injection molding of polystyrene or ABS. Its thin shape and personal hygiene function mean it is not targeted by recycling streams for plastic bottles and flasks. It must consequently be discarded with general household waste to prevent contamination of packaging sorting processes.

common.name: Disque compact rayé

badly damaged: 🔴 A CD broken into several sharp pieces, its metal reflective layer peeling off.
🔴 This disc shows a blistered reflective surface yellowed by water exposure.
🔴 A fragment of compact disc, opaque and flaked by aquatic erosion.
damaged: 🟠 A compact disc with deep circular scratches on its readable side.
🟠 This CD shows sediment traces on its edges and minor chemical fading.
🟠 A worn disc displaying chips around its plastic central hole.
worn: 🟡 A whole compact disc showing minor superficial scratches.
🟡 This CD shows superficial moisture without peeling of its reflective backing.
🟡 A worn compact disc discarded recently, its printed label still legible.

common.fun_fact: The compact disc is composed of ninety-nine percent clear polycarbonate coated with a thin reflective layer of aluminum or gold protected by an acrylic lacquer. This intimate mix of metals and stable technical plastic makes its recycling complex, requiring its disposal in general household waste in the absence of specialized electronics collection.

common.name: Cassette audio usagée

badly damaged: 🔴 A broken plastic audio cassette with its magnetic tape tangled all around.
🔴 This clear plastic housing is yellowed by the sun and filled with silt.
🔴 A clump of crumpled brown magnetic tape stuck in dark river mud.
damaged: 🟠 An audio cassette with cracks on its plastic shell and embedded sand.
🟠 This housing shows torn paper labels and small rusted assembly screws.
🟠 A worn cassette displaying loose, twisted magnetic tape spilling out.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn audio cassette, its magnetic tape neatly wound.
🟡 This plastic housing shows minor surface scratches without major breakage.
🟡 A music cassette discarded recently, still clean despite ambient moisture.

common.fun_fact: The audio cassette combines a rigid polystyrene housing with a long polyester tape coated with magnetic particles of iron oxide, chromium, or cobalt, plus plastic rollers. This combination of magnetic metals and flexible polymers cannot be recycled and must be deposited in the unsorted household waste containers.

common.name: Pince à linge cassée

badly damaged: 🔴 A half-clothespin of plastic faded by the sun and broken in two.
🔴 This piece of plastic shows a rusted and twisted metal spring.
🔴 A fragment of rigid, curved plastic, the remains of a broken clothespin.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic clothespin with cracked or deformed gripping jaws.
🟠 This model shows rust on its tension spring and embedded sand grains.
🟠 A worn clothespin showing chemical discoloration and small cracks.
worn: 🟡 A clothespin whole but broken at the spring level, recently discarded.
🟡 This plastic model shows minor surface scratches without major breakage.
🟡 A worn clothespin whose plastic is slightly dull but otherwise intact.

common.fun_fact: The plastic clothespin is made of a molded polypropylene body and a galvanized steel tension spring. Its small size and the inseparable presence of the metal spring prevent it from being recycled in automated packaging sorting centers, requiring its final disposal with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Lime à ongles usée

badly damaged: 🔴 A cardboard nail file broken into several soft, peeling pieces.
🔴 This tool shows a completely worn-down abrasive surface covered in mud.
🔴 A split rigid cardboard fragment whose glass-grit layer has peeled off.
damaged: 🟠 A cardboard nail file bent and dirty, displaying tears along its edges.
🟠 This file shows frayed borders and embedded fine sand grains.
🟠 A worn manicure tool displaying dark moisture stains on its surface.
worn: 🟡 A nail file whole but worn, showing minor surface wrinkles.
🟡 This model shows minor traces of use without cracks in the cardboard.
🟡 A cardboard file discarded recently, still rigid despite ambient moisture.

common.fun_fact: The disposable nail file is manufactured from a core of rigid cardboard or flexible plastic, coated with a synthetic abrasive like emery or corundum powder glued with resins. This complex and hardened abrasive composite cannot be processed for recycling and must be discarded with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Sac poubelle déchiré

badly damaged: 🔴 A black trash bag torn into shapeless shreds, full of foul-smelling silt.
🔴 This thin plastic is shredded into small pieces stuck together by mud.
🔴 Filaments of black plastic wrapped tightly around wet wooden debris.
damaged: 🟠 A trash bag with major tears and embedded sand grains.
🟠 This plastic shows heavy stress wrinkles and has lost its original dark color.
🟠 A used black bag displaying folding lines and greasy stains.
worn: 🟡 A black trash bag whole but worn, recently discarded after use.
🟡 This bag shows minor tension marks without any major holes.
🟡 A crumpled and damp black plastic bag, floating near the water edge.

common.fun_fact: Black trash bags are made of recycled low-density polyethylene, but their extreme thinness and dark color mean they are not targeted for further recycling. Additionally, they are used to contain soiled waste, which excludes any hygienic sorting options and requires their final thermal disposal alongside general household waste.

common.name: Bouchon de dentifrice

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic toothpaste cap split in two, yellowed and covered in silt.
🔴 This cylindrical cap shows bite marks and disintegrates when handled.
🔴 A rigid plastic residue blocked by a hard aggregate of sand and clay.
damaged: 🟠 A toothpaste cap with dried white paste remaining on the inside.
🟠 This cap shows sand rubbing scratches and persistent limescale deposits.
🟠 A worn plastic cap displaying minor deformations on its screw thread.
worn: 🟡 A toothpaste cap whole but worn, displaying light surface scratches.
🟡 This model shows minor traces of use without any visible cracks.
🟡 A white plastic cap discarded recently, still relatively clean.

common.fun_fact: The toothpaste tube cap is manufactured from rigid polypropylene. Due to its very small size and the frequent presence of dried cleaning paste residues, it is not sorted by automated packaging recycling centers and must be disposed of with general waste alongside normal non-recyclable trash to avoid clogging machinery.

common.name: Papier de verre usé

badly damaged: 🔴 A sheet of sandpaper broken into soft, peeling pieces with grains detaching.
🔴 This abrasive paper is torn and covered in damp black mold.
🔴 A fragment of split abrasive sheet whose grit has dissolved in the water.
damaged: 🟠 A sandpaper sheet warped by moisture, with wood dust stains.
🟠 This paper shows frayed edges and fine sand grains embedded on it.
🟠 A worn abrasive sheet displaying significant cracks in the cardboard base.
worn: 🟡 A sandpaper sheet whole but worn, displaying light surface scratches.
🟡 This model shows minor traces of use without cracks in the cardboard backing.
🟡 A sandpaper sheet discarded recently, still rigid despite ambient moisture.

common.fun_fact: Sandpaper, or emery paper, is a composite made of a heavy paper or cloth backing, a strong synthetic adhesive, and abrasive grains of silicon carbide or alumina. This mixture of insoluble binders and hard minerals prohibits any recycling of its structure, requiring its disposal with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Brosse à mascara usée

badly damaged: 🔴 A mascara wand with its plastic stem broken and bristles torn off.
🔴 This brush body is covered in dried black makeup and dark silt.
🔴 A split rigid brush handle, the remains of a faded cosmetic brush.
damaged: 🟠 A mascara brush with synthetic bristles clumped together by black residues.
🟠 This wand shows gray wear marks and persistent limescale deposits.
🟠 A worn makeup tool whose plastic brush head is cracked or chipped.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn mascara wand, its bristles slightly bent or warped.
🟡 This model shows minor signs of use without cracks on its stem handle.
🟡 A recently discarded mascara brush, still black with cosmetic product.

common.fun_fact: The mascara brush is made of very dense twisted nylon threads set on a stainless steel wire stem or molded elastomers, all fitted to a plastic cap. This complex cosmetic tool full of insoluble waxes and black pigments cannot be recycled, requiring its final disposal with general non-recyclable household waste.

common.name: Restes de feux d'artifice

badly damaged: 🔴 Charred cardboard tubes reduced to soft pieces full of black soot.
🔴 This heavy cardboard casing is ruptured, revealing gray sulfur deposits.
🔴 Shreds of heavy cardboard blackened by fire, its structure decomposing.
damaged: 🟠 A firework mortar tube crumpled and blackened on the inside by gunpowder.
🟠 This casing shows major tears along its walls and damp, soiled areas.
🟠 A worn firework remnant showing residues of hardened clay plugging.
worn: 🟡 A whole but spent firework tube, dull brown in color with soot stains.
🟡 This thick cardboard casing shows superficial burns without major holes.
🟡 A firework remnant discarded recently, dry on its heavy cardboard base.

common.fun_fact: Spent fireworks consist of heavy cardboard impregnated with alkaline combustion residues, charcoal, color-producing heavy metals like barium or strontium, and clay plugs. This toxic, non-recyclable chemical cocktail must be disposed of via standard non-recyclable streams to prevent direct environmental pollution and chemical runoff.

common.name: Briquet jetable vide

badly damaged: 🔴 A split plastic lighter body, missing all metal parts and yellowed by water.
🔴 This lighter shows a spark wheel that is completely rusted and seized.
